content.text: Writing and speaking are the two ways practitioners make their work visible beyond their immediate team. A blog post reaches hundreds of people who will never interact with your GitHub profile. A conference talk reaches an audience of practitioners who self-selected to care about your topic. Both compound: the people who read your post remember your name when a role opens up; the people who saw your talk recommend you when a colleague needs someone with your specialization.
Why Write at All
Writing about technical work has benefits that are independent of the audience:

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Writing reveals gaps.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' The moment you try to explain your approach to an imagined reader, the parts you do not fully understand become visible. Writing is the most efficient debugging tool for your own understanding.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Writing creates reference material.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' A decision you documented six months ago is available to you six months from now. "Why did we choose X over Y?" is answerable in 30 seconds when you wrote it down.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Writing generates professional leverage.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' A post about a non-obvious problem that 50 practitioners share reaches all of them simultaneously. A conversation at a conference reaches three.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Writing forces specificity.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Vague impressions that a technique "seemed to work" cannot survive the discipline of writing a clear claim with supporting evidence.'}
The post you're afraid to publish because it might be wrong is usually the most valuable one. It starts a conversation. The safe, summary post no one needed to write is the one that gets ignored.

attrs.alt: Blog post structure and the content-to-talk progression from experiment to conference

content.text: What to Write About
The most common block is believing you have nothing worth writing about. In practice, the highest-value technical posts come from two sources:

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Something you spent more than four hours debugging.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' If it took you four hours, it will take the next person four hours too. A 500-word post with the root cause and fix saves that time for everyone who finds it.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'A decision you made where the obvious choice turned out to be wrong.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' These posts teach judgment, not just facts. "We tried X because everyone recommends it for this use case, and here is what actually happened" is the highest-value blog content in any technical field.'}
Topics that consistently perform well in GenAI:
Topic Type
Example Title Pattern
Why It Works
Failure report
"Why our RAG pipeline returned wrong answers 15% of the time"
Honest failure reporting is rare; practitioners trust it
Unexpected finding
"We expected fine-tuning to win; prompt engineering was better"
Counterintuitive results make people read and share
Comparison with numbers
"GPT-4o vs Claude 3.5 on our legal extraction task"
Specific benchmarks on real tasks are always in demand
Solved hard problem
"How we reduced hallucination rate from 8% to 1.2%"
Concrete improvement with a method is immediately actionable
Architecture decision
"Why we chose a hybrid search over pure vector search"
Decision rationale with tradeoffs teaches judgment
Blog Post Structure That Gets Read
Most technical blog posts lose readers in the first three paragraphs by front-loading context instead of the point. A structure that works:
The Hook (First 150 Words)

State the problem, why you thought it would be easy, and the unexpected finding. Do not start with a history of the field, the definition of a term, or why your company decided to work on this. Start with the moment the problem became real:

## Bad opening (buried lede):
"Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has become a popular approach for grounding
large language model outputs in external knowledge sources. In this post, we will
explore how our team implemented RAG for our legal document review system..."

## Good opening (immediate hook):
"Our RAG pipeline was returning confident, well-formatted answers that were
factually wrong 15% of the time — and we didn't know it for six weeks.
Here's what caused it and how we fixed it.

The problem wasn't the retrieval or the LLM. It was a chunking decision
we made in the first hour of the project and never revisited."
The Evidence Section (400–800 Words)

Show your work. Numbers are more trustworthy than adjectives. "Latency improved significantly" is a claim. "P99 latency dropped from 2.4s to 0.8s after switching from 512-token chunks to sentence-boundary chunks" is evidence. Include:
What you measured and how (not just the final number)
What you tried that did not work, and the specific failure mode
Working code that reproduces the key result — not pseudocode
Explicit scope: what data distribution, what model, what scale

# Include runnable code that reproduces the key result
# This is the single most-cited part of any technical post

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

# The chunking strategy that was silently causing our hallucinations:
bad_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,          # arbitrary token count
    chunk_overlap=50,
    separators=["\n\n", "\n", " ", ""],  # splits mid-sentence
)

# The fix — sentence-boundary aware chunking:
from langchain_text_splitters import SentenceTransformersTokenTextSplitter
good_splitter = SentenceTransformersTokenTextSplitter(
    chunk_overlap=0,
    tokens_per_chunk=256,    # smaller but semantically complete
)

# Benchmark result:
# bad_splitter:  answer_accuracy=0.847, hallucination_rate=0.153
# good_splitter: answer_accuracy=0.978, hallucination_rate=0.022
# Improvement achieved with no change to the LLM or retrieval model.
The Takeaway (Last 100 Words)

State what changed in your practice as a result of this investigation, and what you still do not know. Ending with an open question invites comments and continued discussion. Do not summarize what you already said — the reader just read it.
Where to Publish
Platform
Best For
Discoverability
Personal blog (GitHub Pages / Hugo)
Control over content and format; permanent URL
Low — requires active sharing; SEO takes months
Substack
Building a subscriber list; newsletter format
Medium — subscriber notifications; growing ML audience
Medium / Towards Data Science
Existing ML audience; free syndication
High — TDS has large ML readership; Medium surfaces to followers
Hugging Face Blog
Model releases, dataset releases, research results
Very high for ML practitioners — Hugging Face audience is ideal
Company engineering blog
Credibility signal; team-reviewed
Variable — depends on company's audience and promotion

The best strategy for most practitioners: write on a personal blog for permanent URL ownership, then cross-post to Medium/Towards Data Science for discovery. Keep the canonical URL on your own domain.
Giving Talks

A talk is a blog post with a live audience and no backspace key. The preparation discipline required to give a 20-minute technical talk is higher than for a blog post, but the payoff — the conversations that happen before and after the talk — is qualitatively different from what any written post produces.
Starting with Internal and Local Talks
The progression matters. Speaking at a company all-hands or a local meetup is fundamentally different from speaking at a major conference, and the skills transfer:

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Internal team demo day or tech talk.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': " Zero stakes. Practice the flow, get questions you hadn't anticipated, and discover which parts of the explanation are unclear."}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Local AI meetup.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' 20–50 person audience. Questions are genuine and the bar for polish is low. This is where most practitioners should start with public speaking.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Regional or topic-specific conference.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' PyData, local ML conferences, applied ML tracks. A 20-minute talk slot, submitted as a proposal, reviewed by a program committee.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Major conference workshop.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' NeurIPS, ICML, ACL workshops accept practitioner talks on applied topics. The bar is lower than the main track and the audience is specifically interested in your subdomain.'}
Talk Structure for Technical Audiences
A 20-minute technical talk has a different optimal structure than a blog post. The audience cannot scroll back:

## 20-Minute Technical Talk Structure

### 0:00–2:00 — The Problem (2 min)
What specific problem, for whom, at what scale?
The problem must be concrete enough that the audience can assess whether it applies to them.

### 2:00–4:00 — Why This Is Hard (2 min)
What obvious approach fails and why?
Establishes that you did not take an easy shortcut and that the problem is real.

### 4:00–14:00 — What We Did (10 min)
Walk through the approach with 2–3 slides of results.
At least one slide must show numbers. At least one must show a failure case.
Include the decision point where you chose this approach over an alternative.

### 14:00–17:00 — Results and Scope (3 min)
What improved? What didn't? What data distribution / scale does this apply to?
Be explicit about what you did NOT test.

### 17:00–20:00 — Q&A setup (3 min)
End with an open question to invite discussion.
Leave 3 minutes for questions — the Q&A is often the most valuable part.
Preparing for Questions
Questions you will always get at GenAI practitioner talks:
"How did this scale to production traffic?" — know your traffic numbers
"Did you compare against [other model / approach]?" — know what you didn't test and why
"What was the cost?" — know your approximate cost per request
"What happens when the model is updated?" — know your eval coverage
"Is this open source?" — be ready with the GitHub URL or a clear explanation of why not
Preparing answers to these five questions covers 80% of post-talk Q&A for applied GenAI topics.
SEO and Distribution for Technical Posts
A technically excellent post that no one reads has no career impact. Distribution is not an afterthought — it is part of the work:
Title and First Paragraph for Discoverability

Search engine discovery for technical posts is driven primarily by the specificity of the title. A title that matches the exact words a practitioner would type when searching for their problem ranks; a clever title does not:
Weak Title
Strong Title
Why
"Adventures in RAG"
"Why RAG Pipelines Hallucinate on Numerical Data (and How We Fixed It)"
Specific problem + solution signal; matches search intent
"Thoughts on Fine-Tuning"
"LoRA vs Full Fine-Tuning: Results on a 7B Model with 2K Training Examples"
Concrete comparison with specific parameters; practitioners search for exactly this
"LLMs in Production"
"Reducing LLM Inference Latency by 40% with Speculative Decoding on vLLM"
Measurable outcome + specific tool; high intent query
Sharing Without Spamming
Post your work where it is relevant, not everywhere:

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Hacker News: ', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': '{\'type\': \'text\', \'text\': \'Show HN:\', \'marks\': [{\'type\': \'code\'}]} {\'type\': \'text\', \'text\': " prefix for demos/tools; plain link for analysis posts. Post when you have something you actually built, not a summary of others\' work."}'}
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Reddit r/MachineLearning:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Posts about original experiments or findings. Tutorial reproductions are not welcome.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Twitter/X and LinkedIn:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' A three-sentence summary + link. The summary is the hook — do not make people click to find out what the post is about.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Relevant Discord/Slack communities:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Only share in communities where the topic is specifically relevant. Sharing in five unrelated channels is spam; sharing in the one community where it belongs is valuable.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Direct outreach:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': " If a post directly addresses someone's open question in a GitHub issue or forum thread, link to it. This is the highest-conversion distribution method."}
Responding to Comments
The comment section is the highest-leverage part of a successful post. Responding to every substantive comment in the first 48 hours:
Surfaces errors in your reasoning before they propagate — readers correct mistakes faster than you would catch them alone
Generates follow-up post ideas — the most common questions become the next post
Builds relationships with commenters who often become collaborators or referrers
Getting Your First Speaking Slot
The first external talk is the hardest to get because you have no speaking record. Practical paths to the first slot:

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Local AI/ML meetup cold submission.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Most local meetups accept proposals from unknown speakers with a title, three-sentence abstract, and GitHub profile. The bar is low — organizers need speakers more than speakers need to be famous.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Company sponsorship.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Many companies have a speaker program that covers conference fees for employees giving external talks. The company gets a logo placement; you get a speaking slot. This is the easiest path to a regional conference talk.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Conference workshop + poster.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Workshops at major conferences (NeurIPS, ICML) often have poster sessions that accept applied work that would not make the main track. A poster is a conversation, not a lecture, and is a legitimate entry point.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Invited talk via blog post.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' A well-distributed blog post gets noticed by meetup and conference organizers. The most efficient path to a speaking slot is often: write the post → be approached by an organizer who read it.'}

Speaker proposals that get accepted share a common structure: a specific claim, evidence that you have data to support it, and a clear audience — "practitioners running LLM pipelines in production" is better than "anyone interested in AI". Organizers are making a bet on whether your talk will satisfy their specific audience.
Writing and Speaking as a Compounding Practice

The value of writing and speaking compounds over time in ways that individual effort does not. A post written in 2024 that ranks for a relevant search query continues generating inbound in 2026. A talk that gets posted to YouTube is a permanent piece of your portfolio. The people who read or watch your work join your network passively — they reach out when they have a relevant problem or opportunity.
Practical cadence that is sustainable:

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'One blog post per quarter.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Four posts per year is enough to maintain a visible writing presence. Trying to write monthly produces burnout or low-quality filler.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'One internal talk per quarter.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' A team demo or lunch-and-learn that takes 1 hour to prepare builds the habit of public explanation without conference submission overhead.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'One external talk per year.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' A single meetup or regional conference talk annually is achievable for most practitioners and builds a speaking track record that makes the next submission easier to accept.'}
A practitioner who writes one honest technical post every three months for five years has a body of work that speaks louder than any resume section.
Common Writing Anti-Patterns in Technical GenAI Posts
Anti-pattern
What It Signals
Fix
Summarizing a paper without adding perspective
You read it; you did not learn from it
Add your implementation result, where it failed, or how it changes your practice
Tutorial that exactly follows the official docs
You can read; you cannot apply
Use the technique on a real problem and report what changed
"We achieved SOTA" without defining the benchmark
Benchmark-chasing instead of problem-solving
Describe the real-world task and why the metric matters for that task
Long context before any claim
Writing to appear thorough, not to communicate
Lead with the claim; put context after for readers who want it
Advice posts without evidence
Opinion dressed as expertise
Every recommendation needs a "we measured" or "we found" anchor

The failure mode most damaging to credibility is the post that presents a tutorial result as production experience. Readers who try to apply it in a different context quickly discover the gap. If your post describes an experiment, say so. If it describes a system in production, say what scale and what edge cases you have seen.
Using AI Assistance in Your Writing Workflow

LLMs can improve the efficiency of writing technical posts without compromising the intellectual honesty that makes technical writing valuable. The key distinction: use AI to improve your writing, not to replace your thinking.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Outline review:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Paste your outline and ask "what important questions would a practitioner still have after reading this?" — surfaces gaps before you write 2,000 words.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Clarity editing:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Paste a paragraph and ask "what is unclear about this explanation for a reader who knows Python but is new to RAG?" — better than asking for a rewrite.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Title generation:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Give the LLM your one-sentence summary and ask for five title variations optimized for search intent — then pick and edit the best one.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Code comment generation:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' AI-generated comments on working code you wrote are fine. AI-generated code that you present as evidence of working research is not.'}

The boundary that matters: the experiment, the measurement, the failure analysis, and the interpretation must be yours. The prose style, the structure, and the clarity can be improved with AI assistance without compromising the post's intellectual value.

A post written with AI assistance that contains your original experiment is more valuable than a post written entirely by hand that summarizes someone else's work. The source of value is the thinking, not the typing.
Summary

Writing and speaking turn private learning into public value and professional network. High-signal blog posts start with the hook — the unexpected finding or failure — and support claims with numbers, working code, and explicit scope limitations. The content-to-talk progression — experiment → private draft → internal sharing → local talk → conference — de-risks each step using the previous one. A sustainable cadence of one post and one external talk per year is more valuable over a decade than bursts of activity followed by years of silence.
